item.title: HTTP/2 Bomb, TA4922 Atlas RAT, and VS Code GitHub Token Theft
item.description: This 24-hour digest is dominated by exploitable infrastructure and application weaknesses, active malware distribution, and expanding abuse of trusted platforms. Reporting also highlighted continued pressure on critical infrastructure, identity systems, developer tooling, and cryptocurrency payment...
item.content:encoded:
This 24-hour digest is dominated by exploitable infrastructure and application weaknesses, active malware distribution, and expanding abuse of trusted platforms. Reporting also highlighted continued pressure on critical infrastructure, identity systems, developer tooling, and cryptocurrency payment rails used by threat actors. Researchers disclosed HTTP/2 Bomb, a denial-of-service technique affecting major web servers and proxies including NGINX, Apache HTTPD, IIS, Envoy, and Cloudflare Pingora. The attack combines HPACK compression amplification with Slowloris-style flow-control stalling, allowing a single client to consume large amounts of server memory in seconds under default HTTP/2 configurations. Fixes were reported for NGINX 1.29.8 and Apache modhttp2 2.0.41 under CVE-2026-49975, while some affected platforms remained without patches at publication time. CISA added two exploited vulnerabilities to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog: CVE-2025-48595 in Android Framework and CVE-2022-0492 in Linux cgroups v1. Separate research detailed CVE-2026-23479, a Redis use-after-free flaw introduced in Redis 7.2.0 that can allow authenticated command execution on the database host. Acer also confirmed two maximum-severity zero-days in Wave 7 mesh routers, including unauthenticated credential exposure via CVE-2026-49200 and persistent backdoor injection via CVE-2026-49201. Multiple reports centered on token theft and authentication boundary failures. A one-click GitHub.dev and VS Code webview attack can steal broad GitHub OAuth tokens by simulating keypresses, installing a malicious extension, and extracting tokens that may provide access across all repositories available to the user. The issue was reported as unpatched at publication time and does not affect VS Code Desktop. Microsoft 365 Android apps including Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Copilot, Loop, and OneNote were found to have shipped with a leftover debug flag that disabled a check limiting token sharing to trusted Microsoft applications. Separately, researchers disclosed an unpatched Windows search: URI issue that can trigger Net-NTLMv2 hash leakage via attacker-controlled SMB connections, similar in mechanism to CVE-2026-33829. Proofpoint reported sharply increased activity from TA4922, a Chinese-speaking financially motivated group now targeting entities in Germany, Italy, the United Kingdom, and South Africa after prior focus on East Asia. The group uses localized phishing themes and messaging platforms such as WhatsApp, LINE, and Microsoft Teams, and has deployed Atlas RAT, RomulusLoader, SilentRunLoader, and Winos4.0. Other campaigns abused trusted or high-traffic ecosystems. A malspam operation used Google DoubleClick click-tracking URLs to redirect victims to a personalized phishing page delivering DesckVB RAT through JavaScript, PowerShell, and .NET loaders. Check Point described a malware distribution ecosystem impersonating open-source and freeware projects, including security tools, with redirect chains leading to SessionGate, RemusStealer, and AnimateClipper. Gaming and piracy-themed lures remained prominent. Kaspersky reported Argamal RAT hidden in hentai games distributed through dedicated sites, PixelDrain, and torrents, while McAfee described Weedhack malware targeting Minecraft users through SEO poisoning and YouTube promotion. Additional reporting covered CountLoader infections estimated at 86,000 machines and cryptocurrency miners distributed through fake video player updates on illegal streaming sites. CISA, the FBI, NSA, Department of Energy, and partner agencies warned that attackers are targeting internet-exposed automatic tank gauge systems used to monitor fuel and liquid storage. Reported compromises involve command execution and settings changes affecting network configuration, product identifiers, tank volumes, pump controls, and operational alerts. SafeBreach research showed how poisoned notifications from apps such as WhatsApp, Slack, SMS, Signal, Instagram, and Messenger could hijack Google Gemini’s Android Utilities feature. The demonstrated prompt-injection bypass enabled actions including fake messages, smart home control, URL opening, Zoom call initiation, memory poisoning, and persistent scheduled actions; Google said server-side classifier changes mitigated the issue in November 2025. Google also began rolling out Android fake call detection intended to identify AI-based impersonation calls when supported Google communications apps and RCS are in use. The U.S. Treasury sanctioned Nobitex, Iran’s largest cryptocurrency exchange, citing facilitation of payments tied to terrorist activity and identifying wallets associated with IRGC-linked ransomware actors. The wider Economic Fury campaign also targeted Wallex, Bitpin, and Ramzinex, while Chainalysis estimated that Nobitex processed more than half of Iranian crypto inflows in 2025. European and international law enforcement dismantled nine organized crime groups in Operation KRATOS 2, targeting illegal streaming infrastructure across 13 countries and removing more than 27,000 URLs. In U.S. policy developments, DHS leadership signaled plans to reshape CISA amid workforce and budget reductions, while a commission estimated that establishing a dedicated U.S. Cyber Force could cost up to $11 billion and require around 30,000 personnel.

Autonomous AI Tool Finds 2-Year-Old RCE Flaw in Redis (CVE-2026-23479) — Must Read

Redis patched CVE-2026-23479, a two-year-old authenticated use-after-free flaw that can lead to remote code execution and was discovered by an autonomous AI bug-hunting tool. Users should upgrade to fixed Redis versions immediately, especially for internet-exposed or weakly authenticated deployments.

  • Redis patched CVE-2026-23479, a use-after-free flaw in its blocking-client code that can let an authenticated user run arbitrary OS commands on the database host.
  • The flaw was introduced in Redis 7.2.0 and remained in every stable branch until fixes were released on May 5.
  • The vulnerability was found by Team Xint Code using Xint Code, an autonomous AI security tool built to hunt bugs in large codebases.
  • The published exploit chain leaks a heap pointer with Lua, reclaims freed client memory with a fake client structure, and overwrites a function pointer to execute shell commands.
  • Redis users are advised to upgrade to fixed versions 7.2.14, 7.4.9, 8.2.6, 8.4.3, or 8.6.3, and to restrict ACLs if they cannot patch immediately.

CISA warns of cyberattacks targeting fuel tank monitoring systems — Must Read

CISA and other U.S. agencies warn that hackers are compromising internet-exposed automatic tank gauge systems used to monitor fuel and liquid storage tanks, potentially disrupting readings, alerts, and pump controls. Operators should remove these systems from direct internet access, harden authentication, patch them, and monitor for unauthorized changes.

  • CISA, the FBI, the NSA, the Department of Energy, and other U.S. government partners warned that hackers are targeting internet-exposed automatic tank gauge systems used to monitor fuel and liquid storage tanks.
  • The agencies said attackers are compromising exposed ATG systems and modifying system settings through command execution.
  • Attackers are gaining access through authentication bypass vulnerabilities, hardcoded credentials, operating system command-execution flaws, SQL injection vulnerabilities, and privilege-escalation weaknesses.
  • A successful compromise could allow attackers to alter network settings, product identifiers, tank volumes, and pump controls, or turn off alerts that help operators monitor tank fill levels.
  • The agencies urged organizations to block ATG systems from the internet, restrict remote access, replace default passwords, use strong credentials and multifactor authentication, apply updates, and monitor for unauthorized changes.

CISA warns of active attacks exploiting Android, Linux bugs — Must Read

CISA says attackers are actively exploiting two high-severity flaws: an Android Framework privilege escalation bug affecting Android 14–16 and a Linux kernel cgroups v1 flaw that can enable container escape and root access. Federal agencies must apply fixes or mitigations by June 5, and other organizations should patch urgently.

  • CISA added CVE-2025-48595, a high-severity integer overflow vulnerability in the Android Framework that can be leveraged for increased privileges, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog.
  • Google said CVE-2025-48595 affects Android 14 through 16, requires no user interaction to exploit, and may be under limited targeted exploitation in the wild.
  • CISA also added CVE-2022-0492, a high-severity privilege escalation flaw affecting multiple Linux kernel branches from 2.6 through 4.20 and from 5.5 through 5.17, to the KEV catalog.
  • CVE-2022-0492 is in the cgroups v1 subsystem and can allow a local attacker to bypass namespace isolation, escalate privileges, and potentially escape from a container to gain root-level access on the host system.
  • Federal agencies covered by BOD 22-01 must apply vendor-provided updates and mitigations for the two flaws or stop using the impacted software by June 5.

Impersonation, Click Hijacking, and TDS: Inside a Malware Distribution Ecosystem — Should Read

Check Point Research found a large malware distribution ecosystem using fake open-source and freeware download sites that preserve legitimate-looking links but hijack the first click into a gated traffic distribution system. Depending on filtering and user context, victims were routed to payloads including SessionGate, RemusStealer, and AnimateClipper.

  • Check Point Research investigated a large-scale operation that impersonates open-source and freeware projects, including security tools such as Ghidra, dnSpy, and SpiderFoot, to capture search traffic.
  • The fake sites load CloudFront-hosted JavaScript that can intercept a user's first download click and hand the session to a gated Traffic Distribution System with anti-bot, anti-analysis, VPN/datacenter filtering, and frequency-capping logic.
  • Observed redirect chains sometimes led to benign software or potentially unwanted applications, but other branches delivered malware families including SessionGate, RemusStealer, and AnimateClipper.
  • SessionGate uses multi-stage delivery, per-session payload generation, server-side validation, one-time-style key release behavior, and heavy obfuscation to make final payload retrieval and analysis difficult.
  • RemusStealer targets browser data, cryptocurrency wallet extensions, password managers, 2FA extensions, clipboard contents, screenshots, files, and registry data through encrypted C2-driven tasking.

New 'HTTP/2 Bomb' DoS attack crashes web servers in under a minute — Should Read

Researchers disclosed “HTTP/2 Bomb,” a DoS technique that can crash major web servers by exhausting tens of gigabytes of memory from a single machine in seconds. NGINX and Apache have patches, but IIS, Envoy, and Cloudflare Pingora lacked fixes at the time, making mitigations like disabling HTTP/2 or enforcing header limits important.

  • A new denial-of-service technique called HTTP/2 Bomb can be launched from a single machine to take down vulnerable web servers within seconds.
  • HTTP/2 Bomb combines HPACK compression amplification with Slowloris-style resource retention via HTTP/2 flow-control stalling.
  • Researchers reported that a single client can consume and hold 32 GB of server memory in about 10 seconds on Envoy 1.37.2 and about 18 seconds on Apache httpd 2.4.67.
  • The issue was fixed in nginx 1.29.8 with a new max_headers directive and in Apache httpd mod_http2 2.0.41 under CVE-2026-49975.
  • At the time of writing, no patch was available for IIS, Envoy, or Cloudflare Pingora, and researchers recommended disabling HTTP/2 where feasible or placing a proxy or firewall in front to enforce hard header-count limits.

Microsoft 365 Android Apps Let Any App Steal Account Tokens via Leftover Debug Flag — Should Read

Several Microsoft 365 Android apps shipped with a leftover debug flag that let any app on the same device steal signed-in users’ account tokens and access email, files, calendars, and messages. Microsoft has patched the flaw, but affected users should update immediately and consider revoking refresh tokens if untrusted apps were present.

  • A development flag left enabled in production builds of several Microsoft 365 Android apps disabled a check that limited account-token sharing to trusted Microsoft apps.
  • The flaw affected Word, PowerPoint, Excel, Microsoft 365 Copilot, Microsoft Loop, and OneNote, while Teams was not affected because it shipped with the flag set to false.
  • Enclave researchers Yanir Tsarimi and Ofek Levin found that the check was skipped because setIsDebugMode(true) was left in a shared Microsoft SDK used by multiple apps.
  • Microsoft issued CVEs on May 12 for Microsoft 365 Copilot, Word, PowerPoint, and Excel, and the affected apps were patched through Google Play updates.
  • The patch does not retroactively revoke tokens an attacker may already hold, so the article recommends revoking refresh tokens and forcing a fresh sign-in for accounts on devices that ran old builds alongside untrusted apps.

Chinese hackers use new Atlas RAT malware in European cyberattacks — Should Read

Chinese-speaking cybercrime group TA4922 is expanding into Europe with fast-moving phishing campaigns that deploy new malware including Atlas RAT, RomulusLoader, and SilentRunLoader. Although financially motivated, the tools provide surveillance-grade capabilities that could also be used or sold for espionage.

  • Proofpoint tracks the Chinese-speaking cybercrime group as TA4922 and says it is financially motivated, targeting networks for fraud, data theft, and the sale of access.
  • TA4922 previously targeted organizations in East Asia but has recently focused on entities in Germany, Italy, the United Kingdom, and South Africa.
  • Since March, TA4922 activity has increased sharply, and since April it has shown unprecedented operational diversity and a high tempo, according to Proofpoint.
  • TA4922 uses localized phishing lures such as payroll notices, tax audits, VAT filings, government compliance notices, invoices, and human resources communications, and also contacts victims through WhatsApp, LINE, and Microsoft Teams.
  • Proofpoint reported that TA4922 has deployed Atlas RAT, RomulusLoader, SilentRunLoader, and Winos4.0, with Atlas RAT supporting reconnaissance, file theft, downloads, keylogging, screenshots, audio and webcam recording, and shutdown or reboot commands.

One-Click GitHub Dev Attack Lets Attackers Steal Full GitHub OAuth Tokens — Should Read

Researchers disclosed a one-click GitHub.dev/VS Code web attack that can install a malicious extension and steal a user’s full GitHub OAuth token, potentially exposing private repositories. Microsoft has acknowledged the issue and says VS Code Desktop is not affected.

  • Cybersecurity researcher Ammar Askar disclosed a one-click attack via Microsoft Visual Studio Code that can steal a user's GitHub token.
  • The issue affects GitHub.dev, a lightweight web-based source code editor that launches a VS Code environment in the browser and receives an OAuth token from github.com.
  • The stolen GitHub OAuth token is not scoped to a single repository and can provide read and write access to all repositories the user can access, including private repositories.
  • The exploit uses malicious JavaScript inside an untrusted VS Code webview to simulate keypresses, open the Command Palette, and install an attacker-controlled extension that extracts the GitHub OAuth token.
  • Microsoft acknowledged the vulnerability and said it is working on a fix, while also clarifying that the issue does not affect VS Code Desktop.

Google DoubleClick Abused in New Malspam Campaign to Deliver DesckVB RAT — Should Read

A new malspam campaign is abusing Google DoubleClick redirects to make phishing links look legitimate and deliver the DesckVB RAT. The attack uses dynamic victim-specific lures, JavaScript and PowerShell loaders, persistence, defense evasion, and process hollowing to gain remote control of infected Windows systems.

  • A new malspam campaign is using Google's DoubleClick domain to evade detection and deliver the .NET-based DesckVB RAT.
  • The attack begins with an HTML attachment in a phishing email that redirects victims through a Google DoubleClick Campaign Manager click-tracking URL to a malicious landing page.
  • The landing page personalizes itself using the victim's email address by dynamically pulling in company branding and location details.
  • Clicking the landing page's "Download PDF" button downloads a ZIP archive that launches a JavaScript loader, PowerShell script, .NET loader, and ultimately the RAT payload.
  • DesckVB RAT communicates with a command-and-control server over raw TCP sockets, performs reconnaissance, configures Microsoft Defender exclusions, patches AMSI and ETW, establishes persistence, and can extract data, run commands, and deploy additional payloads.

Argamal: Malware hidden in hentai games — Should Read

Kaspersky uncovered Argamal, a new RAT campaign hiding in trojanized hentai games that uses PowerShell, COM hijacking, and delayed payload downloads to gain persistence and full remote control of victims’ Windows systems. The campaign has infected hundreds of users, mainly in Russia, Brazil, Germany, and Vietnam.

  • Kaspersky discovered a malware campaign in April 2026 targeting players of hentai games with a previously unknown malicious implant called Argamal.
  • The trojanized games were distributed through dedicated websites, PixelDrain links, and torrent trackers including AniRena.
  • Argamal uses PowerShell scripts and COM hijacking of the Windows Color System Calibration Loader scheduled task to establish persistence and run at user logon.
  • The payload is a RAT that can execute commands, take screenshots, manage files, reboot or shut down the system, control the cursor, and communicate with C2 infrastructure.
  • Kaspersky telemetry showed hundreds of infections, with most victims located in Russia, Brazil, Germany, and Vietnam.

The U.S. sanctions Nobitex crypto exchange used by ransomware — Should Read

The U.S. Treasury sanctioned Nobitex, Iran’s largest cryptocurrency exchange, for allegedly enabling sanctions evasion and facilitating IRGC-linked terrorist and ransomware-related crypto transactions. The action also targets Nobitex leaders and other Iranian exchanges, freezing U.S.-jurisdiction assets and barring U.S. persons from doing business with them.

  • The U.S. Treasury’s Office of Foreign Assets Control sanctioned Nobitex, Iran’s largest cryptocurrency exchange, for facilitating payments related to terrorist activities.
  • U.S. authorities found wallets associated with IRGC-linked ransomware threat actors among Nobitex-related transactions.
  • OFAC also designated Nobitex executives and founders, including chairman Amir Hossein Rad and CEO Seyed Ali Khoee.
  • The U.S. government’s “Economic Fury” campaign also targeted three other Iranian cryptocurrency exchanges: Wallex, Bitpin, and Ramzinex.
  • Chainalysis reported that the Iranian cryptocurrency ecosystem received nearly $7.8 billion in 2025, with Nobitex processing more than half of Iranian crypto inflows.

WhatsApp, Slack Notifications Could Hijack Google Gemini on Android — Should Read

SafeBreach researchers found that a malicious Android notification from apps like WhatsApp or Slack could hijack Google Gemini through prompt injection, tricking it into faking messages, triggering connected actions, joining calls, or poisoning memory without a malicious app installed. Google has patched the server-side issue, and users can reduce exposure by disabling Gemini’s notification access.

  • SafeBreach researcher Or Yair found that a poisoned notification from apps such as WhatsApp, Slack, SMS, Signal, Instagram, or Messenger could have hijacked Google Gemini’s voice assistant on Android.
  • The issue affected Gemini’s Android Utilities feature, which can read and reply to notifications, and did not require a malicious app to be installed on the phone.
  • Yair bypassed Google’s earlier prompt-injection mitigations using a technique called Fake Context Alignment, which made a user’s harmless-sounding confirmation appear to authorize a sensitive action.
  • The demonstrated impacts included faking messages from contacts, controlling smart home devices through Google Home, opening URLs, forcing the phone into a Zoom call, poisoning Gemini’s account-level memory, and creating persistent scheduled actions.
  • Google treated the report as high priority and confirmed on November 14, 2025, that server-side content-classifier improvements mitigated the notification injections and Delayed Tool Invocation bypass.

Unpatched Windows Search URI Vulnerability Lets Attackers Steal NTLMv2 Hashes — Should Read

Researchers disclosed an unpatched Windows Search URI handler issue that can leak a user’s Net-NTLMv2 hash when they click a crafted link, enabling potential NTLM relay attacks. Microsoft declined to patch it, so organizations should block outbound SMB, enforce SMB signing, and disable NTLM where possible.

  • Cybersecurity researchers disclosed an unpatched issue in the Windows search: URI handler that could expose a user's NTLMv2 hash to an attacker.
  • The issue uses the search: URI handler with the crumb=location: parameter to trigger a connection to an attacker-controlled SMB server and produce a Net-NTLMv2 leak.
  • Huntress said the issue has the same NTLM leakage mechanism, prerequisites, and Moderate rating as CVE-2026-33829, which affected the Windows Snipping Tool's ms-screensketch: URI handler.
  • Microsoft declined to address the issue after responsible disclosure on April 15, 2026, stating that only Important and Critical severity cases meet its servicing bar.
  • The article recommends blocking outbound SMB on unnecessary hosts, enforcing SMB signing, and disabling NTLM where applicable.

DHS chief signals efforts to reshape CISA — Should Read

DHS Secretary Markwayne Mullin said the Trump administration plans to reshape and revitalize CISA despite major staffing losses and proposed budget cuts. He signaled a new nominee to lead the agency is coming soon and argued CISA will remain central to federal cybersecurity efforts.

  • Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin said he would revitalize the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency, which has been a frequent target of President Donald Trump’s criticism.
  • CISA has lost roughly one-third of its workforce and had its $3 billion budget reduced during the second Trump administration.
  • President Trump’s fiscal 2027 budget proposes cutting more than $700 million from CISA, raising bipartisan concerns on Capitol Hill.
  • Mullin said CISA probably needs around 2,800 employees, while it currently has about 2,200 personnel and has authority to hire up to 3,400.
  • Mullin said the White House soon plans to nominate a person to run CISA, which has lacked a Senate-confirmed chief since Trump returned to office.

Google adds Android protection against AI deepfake scam calls — Should Read

Google is rolling out Android “fake call detection” to warn users when scammers spoof a contact’s number and use AI voice cloning to impersonate them. The feature will be enabled by default on Android 12+ starting with Pixel devices, but requires Phone by Google, Contacts, and Google Messages with RCS enabled.

  • Google is introducing a new Android security feature called fake call detection that detects and flags calls where scammers may use artificial intelligence to impersonate a user’s personal contacts.
  • The feature is rolling out globally this month to Android 12 and later devices, starting with Pixel devices, and will be enabled by default.
  • Fake call detection works when both the caller and recipient use Phone by Google by sending a silent, encrypted confirmation signal from the caller’s device to the recipient’s device in real time.
  • If the initial confirmation signal is missing, the recipient’s device pings the contact’s actual phone, and if that device confirms it is not placing a call, the recipient receives an on-screen warning to hang up immediately.
  • The feature is built on the Rich Communication Services open standard and only works on Android devices with Phone by Google, Contacts, and Google Messages with RCS enabled installed.

Acer working to patch max severity zero-days in Wave 7 routers — May Read

Acer is preparing firmware fixes for two maximum-severity zero-day flaws in Wave 7 routers that can expose plaintext credentials and enable persistent backdoor access. Until patches arrive, users should disable or restrict remote management and update firmware as soon as the fix is released.

  • Acer confirmed it is working to address two maximum-severity zero-day vulnerabilities affecting Wave 7 mesh routers running firmware version T7c_GBL_1.01.000055 or earlier.
  • CVE-2026-49200 is a broken access control flaw that allows unauthenticated remote attackers to access plaintext web and Telnet credentials stored in the acer_cgi.log file.
  • CVE-2026-49201 involves a hardcoded AES encryption key in the upload.cgi binary that can allow unauthenticated remote attackers to modify system backups and inject a persistent backdoor.
  • Acer said fixes for the vulnerabilities are planned for deployment by the end of June 2026.
  • Acer advised users to disable remote management or restrict Internet remote access to trusted IP addresses until patches are available.

Police dismantles 9 crime groups in illegal streaming crackdown — May Read

Law enforcement agencies across 13 countries dismantled nine organized crime groups behind illegal streaming services, arresting 29 suspects and taking down over 27,000 piracy-related URLs. Europol said the crackdown targeted the broader criminal infrastructure behind the platforms, not just the public-facing websites.

  • European and international law enforcement agencies dismantled nine organized crime groups and arrested 29 suspects during Operation KRATOS 2, a crackdown on illegal streaming operations.
  • Operation KRATOS 2 was coordinated by Bulgaria with Europol's support and involved authorities from 13 countries, including several European nations, the United Kingdom, and the United States.
  • Investigators identified more than 18,000 IP addresses associated with illegal services, 4,370 piracy-linked domains, nearly 400,000 additional URLs flagged for suspension or removal, and more than 126,000 additional infringing objects.
  • The joint action led to the removal of more than 27,000 illegal streaming URLs tied to unauthorized sports, film, and television content.
  • Europol said the operation targeted the wider criminal ecosystem behind illegal streaming services and warned that users of such services face cybersecurity risks including malware infections, spyware, and data theft.

New cyber force would cost up to $11 billion to start, commission says — May Read

A commission of policy experts and former military leaders says creating a dedicated U.S. Cyber Force would cost up to $11 billion, take 12 to 18 months, and require about 30,000 personnel. The proposal comes as lawmakers weigh whether a separate cyber military branch is needed to strengthen U.S. defenses against Russia, China, and other digital threats.

  • A commission report says a U.S. military branch dedicated to cyber warfare would cost up to $11 billion to establish and should include around 30,000 personnel.
  • The proposed Cyber Force could align with the Department of the Army or operate as its own department within the Pentagon, according to the report.
  • The commission said the Cyber Force would take 12 to 18 months to stand up and would include roughly 5,000 National Guard members and up to 6,000 civilians.
  • The report comes as House and Senate lawmakers prepare to craft the annual defense authorization bill, with some seeing a cyber-specific military service as inevitable.
  • The fiscal 2025 defense policy bill also tasked the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine with studying the feasibility of creating a Cyber Force.

What 345 Days of Untested Exposure Looks Like at a Bank — Optional

Annual penetration tests leave banks exposed for long stretches as infrastructure, vendors, and applications change between assessments. The article argues that continuous, change-driven testing is needed to catch real-world issues like exposed third-party banking portals and cross-tenant data leaks before they become regulatory and fraud risks.

  • The article states that a standard annual external penetration test leaves roughly 345 days of operational reality unvalidated.
  • PCI DSS, FFIEC, and NYDFS all reference penetration testing, and the article says these frameworks assume testing happens in response to significant changes rather than only annually.
  • Sprocket testers found an unauthenticated API endpoint on a regional bank’s third-party mortgage origination portal that allowed sequential tenant ID enumeration and exposed organization records for multiple financial institutions.
  • The exposed records included named staff, business email addresses, direct-dial phone numbers, job titles, and an internal staff-attribution code used for borrower submissions.
  • The article argues that continuous external reconnaissance and human testing are needed because vendor-operated subdomains and cross-tenant logic flaws may be missed by annual scoping and automated scanners.

Shrinking the IAM Attack Surface through Identity Visibility and Intelligence Platforms (IVIP) — Optional

Enterprises have a growing identity visibility gap, with nearly half of identity activity occurring outside centralized IAM controls. The article argues that Identity Visibility and Intelligence Platforms like Orchid Security are needed to continuously discover, unify, analyze, and remediate hidden identity risks across applications, machine identities, and AI agents.

  • The article says 46% of enterprise identity activity occurs outside centralized IAM visibility, creating “Identity Dark Matter” that includes unmanaged applications, local accounts, opaque authentication flows, and over-permissioned non-human identities.
  • Gartner’s Identity Visibility and Intelligence Platform category is described as a visibility and observability layer that ingests and unifies IAM data to provide insight into identity events, user-resource relationships, and posture.
  • The article states that a credible IVIP should provide continuous discovery of human and non-human identities, unify fragmented identity data, and use analytics and AI to turn identity signals into security insight.
  • Orchid Security is presented as implementing the IVIP model through application-level discovery, binary analysis, dynamic instrumentation, proprietary audit telemetry, and identity intelligence across managed and unmanaged systems.
  • The article recommends IAM leaders form cross-disciplinary teams, perform risk-quantified gap analysis, implement no-code remediation, use IVIP telemetry during events such as M&A, and audit application-level identity risks.

Weedhack Attacks Minecraft Users, CountLoader Hits 86K, Miners Spread via Pirated Content — Optional

Researchers report multiple malware campaigns abusing gaming, YouTube, cracked software, and pirated streaming sites, with the Minecraft-focused Weedhack MaaS standing out for cheaply offering infostealing and remote access against players. The key risk is that popular “free” mods, software, and media downloads are being used to compromise devices, steal accounts and crypto, and enable harassment or surveillance.

  • McAfee Labs identified a Minecraft-focused malware-as-a-service campaign called Weedhack that has been active since January 2026 and impersonates Minecraft clients and mods to infect users.
  • Weedhack uses SEO poisoning and YouTube videos to drive victims to malicious URLs, and McAfee identified 3,820 unique malicious JAR files and more than 240 distribution URLs tied to the campaign.
  • The Weedhack malware can steal Minecraft session IDs, browser data, cryptocurrency wallet data, and credentials for Discord, Steam, and Telegram, while its paid tier adds remote access features such as webcam access, keylogging, reverse shell execution, and screen sharing.
  • McAfee Labs reported a large-scale CountLoader campaign estimated to have compromised 86,000 unique machines, with the latest attacks deploying cryptocurrency clipper malware that redirects cryptocurrency transactions.
  • Kaspersky described a years-long campaign using illegal movie and TV streaming sites to distribute a cryptocurrency miner through a fake video player plugin update that uses DLL side-loading.

Beyond the Zero-Day: See Your Network Like an Attacker | Webinar with HD Moore — Optional

The article argues that organizations should stop relying solely on patching and assumed network segmentation, and instead map their environments like attackers do to find hidden assets, unintended bridges, and real attack paths. It promotes a webinar with HD Moore on using live asset discovery and attack-path visibility to prioritize the fixes that reduce breach impact.

  • The article argues that organizations cannot rely on patching every zero-day in time and should instead focus on what a breach can reach inside the network.
  • HD Moore, creator of Metasploit and CEO of runZero, will present a live session on viewing network shape from an attacker’s perspective.
  • The article says assumed segmentation can fail because devices may bridge multiple networks, unregistered gear may appear on unexpected segments, and industrial protocol gateways may hide reachable machines.
  • The article contrasts static asset inventories with attacker-focused path mapping that shows how one foothold can reach systems that cause impact.
  • The webinar aims to help attendees find unknown assets, identify segmentation-breaking bridges, map live attack paths, and prioritize remediation on the most important assets and links.

item.title: CVE-2025-48595 Android Zero-Day, WebLogic Exploitation, WeedHack, and AI-Enabled Tooling
item.description: This 24-hour digest is dominated by active exploitation, credential theft, and continued evidence that AI is accelerating both offensive tooling and security governance debates. Reporting also highlights a widening attack surface across mobile devices, developer ecosystems, browsers, consumer...
item.content:encoded:
This 24-hour digest is dominated by active exploitation, credential theft, and continued evidence that AI is accelerating both offensive tooling and security governance debates. Reporting also highlights a widening attack surface across mobile devices, developer ecosystems, browsers, consumer platforms, and collaboration infrastructure. Several stories center on exploitation of known or newly patched vulnerabilities, including an Android Framework zero-day and an older Oracle WebLogic flaw now confirmed as exploited. Separately, malware campaigns targeting gamers, macOS users, and government entities show continued reliance on social engineering, malicious archives, and trusted distribution channels. Google’s June 2026 Android security update addressed 124 vulnerabilities, including CVE-2025-48595, a high-severity Framework privilege escalation flaw reportedly under limited, targeted exploitation. The integer overflow issue affects Android 14, 15, 16, and 16 QPR2 and may allow local escalation of privilege and code execution without user interaction or additional execution privileges. Google released 2026-06-01 and 2026-06-05 patch levels, with the latter covering kernel and third-party chipset components from vendors including Qualcomm, MediaTek, Unisoc, and Imagination Technologies. CISA added Oracle WebLogic Server CVE-2024-21182 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog following evidence of active exploitation. The flaw, patched by Oracle in July 2024, affects WebLogic Server versions 12.2.1.4.0 and 14.1.1.0.0 and can be exploited by an unauthenticated network attacker via T3 or IIOP to compromise affected servers or access critical data. Reporting noted more than 1,500 internet-exposed WebLogic servers running versions identified as vulnerable. The WeedHack campaign has infected 116,464 systems since January through malicious Minecraft mods, clients, cheats, and utilities promoted via YouTube and SEO poisoning. McAfee identified more than 240 distribution URLs and 3,820 unique malicious JAR files tied to the campaign. The stealer targets Minecraft session IDs, browser cookies and saved passwords, cryptocurrency wallets, Discord, Steam, Telegram credentials, and screenshots, while a premium tier adds remote control, webcam access, keylogging, shell access, and file management. Unit 42 reported Operation FlutterBridge, a macOS malvertising campaign delivering FlutterShell, a Flutter-based backdoor masquerading as applications such as PodcastsLounge, PDF-Brain, and PDF-Ninja. Observed samples were signed with valid Apple Developer IDs and passed notarization, and the malware uses a WebView JavaScript-to-native bridge to let operators modify malicious logic hosted externally. Some variants also include AI summarization functionality that sends document content to attacker-controlled infrastructure before forwarding requests to an AI agent. Red Hat removed 32 packages from its distribution pipeline after a compromised GitHub account was used to distribute credential-stealing malware to developers. The packages were downloaded roughly 117,000 times per week, and the malware was described as a variant of the Mini Shai-Hulud self-propagating worm, whose source code was published by TeamPCP in May. Researchers have not confirmed whether TeamPCP conducted the attack or whether another actor reused the released code. Sekoia attributed January 2026 Gamaredon activity against Ukraine to exploitation of WinRAR CVE-2025-8088, a path traversal flaw used to launch GammaPhish. The infection chain retrieves GammaLoad, which fingerprints hosts, updates registry network settings using dead drop resolvers, and fetches VBScript payloads. Delivered payloads include GammaWorm, which spreads through network shares and USB drives using malicious LNK files, and GammaSteel, a modular stealer that exfiltrates selected files to AWS S3 or fallback attacker infrastructure. Researchers also disclosed Operation XENOFISCAL, a spear-phishing campaign likely conducted by the Pakistan-aligned SideCopy group against Afghanistan’s Ministry of Finance and related provincial offices. The campaign uses Pashto-language lure filenames, malicious LNK files, mshta.exe, a compromised Afghan education domain, and obfuscated in-memory JavaScript. The final payload is Xeno RAT 1.8.7, which supports file operations, keylogging, screenshots, clipboard monitoring, and webcam or microphone surveillance. Russia’s FSB accused foreign intelligence services of conducting a large-scale mobile espionage operation against senior Russian officials. The agency alleged that malware was capable of data theft, communications interception, geolocation tracking, contact extraction, and covert audio and video surveillance, but did not name the spyware, describe infection vectors, or provide technical evidence. Sophos reported a threat actor using an AI-built ransomware toolkit that automates Active Directory discovery and supports EDR evasion development. The toolkit included Cobalt Strike profiles, Telegram bot API command-and-control, Python shellcode injection scripts, a Cloudflare Worker redirector, and a Git repository containing an AD discovery panel plus a lab for testing against Sophos, CrowdStrike, and Windows Defender. Sophos said AI was used to accelerate development and testing, not embedded in deployed malware or operating autonomously in victim environments. Other reporting focused on AI’s role in browser-layer threats and vulnerability operations. Push Security described phishing-as-a-service techniques such as ClickFix, InstallFix, ConsentFix, and device code phishing, while noting that short-lived domains, legitimate sites, malvertising, and search results weaken traditional indicator-based detections. A separate article argued that AI-driven exploitation is compressing disclosure-to-exploitation timelines and cited Anthropic’s Project Glasswing update, which said Claude Mythos Preview helped identify more than 10,000 high- or critical-severity vulnerabilities in one month. Instagram users reported account takeovers after attackers allegedly abused Meta’s AI-powered recovery process with AI-generated selfie videos based on target profile photos. Some victims said the method bypassed two-factor authentication and changed account email addresses, affecting high-value handles including @hey, @korn, and an account previously used by the Obama White House team. Meta stated that the issue had been resolved and impacted accounts were being secured. Microsoft investigated a widespread Exchange Online incident affecting mail flow for customers in North America and Germany. The incident, tracked as EX1331830, caused significant delays and some undelivered messages, with users reporting temporary SMTP deferral errors tied to concurrent connection limits and abrupt connection closures. Dashlane disclosed that fewer than 20 personal-plan users had encrypted vaults downloaded after a May 31 brute-force attack targeting account access, two-factor protections, and new device registration. The company said built-in controls triggered temporary suspensions and authentication issues, and that directly notified users were the only ones whose vaults were at risk. Kaspersky GReAT published a passive wardriving assessment across Mexico City, Guadalajara, and Monterrey ahead of the 2026 FIFA World Cup. The study recorded 84,588 wireless signals and found widespread default or MAC-derived SSID naming, open networks representing roughly 10–12% of detected infrastructure, and WPS advertised by 45% of access points.

CISA flags two-year-old Oracle flaw as actively exploited in attacks — Must Read

CISA says a high-severity Oracle WebLogic Server flaw, CVE-2024-21182, is now being actively exploited and has ordered U.S. federal agencies to patch by June 4. All organizations running affected WebLogic versions should apply Oracle’s July 2024 fixes or otherwise mitigate immediately.

  • CISA added CVE-2024-21182, a high-severity Oracle WebLogic Server vulnerability, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after it was reported as actively exploited in attacks.
  • Oracle patched CVE-2024-21182 in July 2024 and said it can allow an unauthenticated attacker with network access via T3 or IIOP to compromise Oracle WebLogic Server.
  • The vulnerability affects Oracle WebLogic Server versions 12.2.1.4.0 and 14.1.1.0.0 and can lead to unauthorized access to critical data or complete access to accessible WebLogic Server data.
  • Shodan tracks more than 1,592 internet-exposed Oracle WebLogic servers running versions identified in the article as vulnerable to CVE-2024-21182 exploits.
  • CISA ordered U.S. federal agencies to patch affected WebLogic servers by midnight on Thursday, June 4, and urged all network defenders to apply vendor mitigations or discontinue use if mitigations are unavailable.

Gamaredon Exploits WinRAR to Deliver GammaWorm and GammaSteel Against Ukraine — Must Read

Russian state-linked Gamaredon is exploiting a WinRAR path traversal flaw to target Ukraine with a modular infection chain that delivers GammaWorm and GammaSteel for propagation, persistence, and data theft. The campaign uses obfuscated scripts, Telegram-based C2 resolution, and cloud exfiltration, suggesting a resilient architecture likely to be reused.

  • Sekoia attributed Gamaredon activity observed in January 2026 to the exploitation of CVE-2025-8088, a WinRAR path traversal flaw, to launch the GammaPhish HTML Application payload.
  • GammaPhish retrieves GammaLoad, an intermediate VBScript downloader used to fingerprint host systems, update registry network configurations through dead drop resolvers, and fetch and execute VBScript payloads from C2 servers.
  • One payload delivered in the infection chain is GammaWorm, a VBScript worm that establishes persistence with scheduled tasks and replaces legitimate directories on network shares and USB drives with malicious LNK files.
  • GammaWorm resolves its command-and-control infrastructure by sending a GET request through curl to a hard-coded public Telegram channel and uses NTFS Alternate Data Streams to conceal core modules.
  • GammaLoad also delivers GammaSteel, a modular information stealer that captures files matching specific extensions and exfiltrates them to an AWS S3 bucket or an attacker-controlled fallback server.

Google fixes one actively exploited Android zero-day, 124 flaws — Must Read

Google’s June 2026 Android security update fixes 124 flaws, including CVE-2025-48595, a high-severity Android Framework zero-day already being used in limited targeted attacks to gain code execution and elevate privileges. Users should install the latest Android patches as soon as they are available for their devices.

  • Google released the June 2026 Android security patches to address 124 vulnerabilities, including one zero-day flaw exploited in targeted attacks.
  • The actively exploited high-severity Android Framework vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2025-48595, can let local attackers gain code execution and escalate privileges on devices running Android 14 or later.
  • Google said there are indications that CVE-2025-48595 may be under limited, targeted exploitation, but it has not shared technical details or information about the attacks.
  • The update fixes 18 critical vulnerabilities across System, Framework, and Qualcomm closed-source components that could enable denial-of-service conditions and privilege escalation on unpatched Android devices.
  • Google issued the 2026-06-01 and 2026-06-05 security patch levels, with Pixel devices receiving updates immediately while other vendors may take longer.

Operation FlutterBridge: macOS Malvertising Campaign Spreads New FlutterShell Backdoor — Must Read

Unit 42 reports that the CL-CRI-1089 cybercrime cluster is using Google malvertising and verified shell companies to distribute FlutterShell, a notarized macOS backdoor disguised as legitimate podcast and PDF apps. Although mainly observed hijacking Chrome for ad fraud, FlutterShell’s WebView-based JavaScript bridge enables dynamic command execution, file access, environment-variable theft, and potential document exfiltration.

  • Unit 42 is tracking Operation FlutterBridge, a macOS malvertising campaign associated with CL-CRI-1089 that delivers a new Flutter-based backdoor called FlutterShell.
  • FlutterShell masquerades as legitimate macOS desktop applications such as PodcastsLounge, PDF-Brain, and PDF-Ninja, and observed samples were signed with valid Apple Developer IDs and passed Apple notarization.
  • FlutterShell uses a WebView-based JavaScript-to-native bridge that lets attackers host and modify malicious logic on external websites without recompiling or redistributing the application.
  • The malware includes backdoor capabilities such as arbitrary shell command execution, file system interaction, and environment variable exfiltration, while observed activity primarily involved hijacking Google Chrome search and new-tab settings for adware behavior.
  • Some PDF-Brain and PDF-Ninja variants include an AI summarization feature that sends document content to an attacker-controlled C2 server before forwarding requests to an AI agent.

Red Hat removes tainted packages after software pipeline compromise — Should Read

Red Hat removed 32 compromised packages from its software pipeline after a hacked GitHub account was used to distribute credential-stealing malware to developers. The incident is part of a broader wave of software supply chain attacks tied to the leaked Mini Shai-Hulud worm code.

  • Red Hat removed dozens of packages from its software distribution pipeline after attackers used a compromised GitHub account to distribute credential-stealing malware to developers.
  • Red Hat said the incident affected 32 packages that were downloaded roughly 117,000 times per week and that no customer actions are required based on current findings.
  • The malware used in the attack was a variant of the Mini Shai-Hulud self-propagating worm, whose source code was published online by the cybercriminal group TeamPCP on May 12.
  • Researchers said it was not immediately clear whether the attack was carried out by TeamPCP or by another actor using the published Mini Shai-Hulud code.
  • The incident follows a series of software supply chain intrusions affecting widely used developer tools, including LiteLLM, axios and TanStack.

AI-built ransomware toolkit automates EDR evasion, AD discovery — Should Read

Sophos found a human-driven ransomware development toolkit that uses AI agents to rapidly build and iterate payloads for Active Directory discovery and EDR evasion. The case shows AI is accelerating how quickly threat actors can turn public security research into working cybercrime tools, even if the malware itself is not AI-operated.

  • Sophos detected a threat actor using an AI-built ransomware attack toolkit that automates Active Directory discovery and helps evade endpoint detection and response solutions.
  • The toolkit included Cobalt Strike profiles, a Telegram bot API-based command-and-control mechanism, Python scripts for shellcode injection into legitimate Windows executables, and a Cloudflare Worker redirector.
  • Sophos found a Git repository containing an automated Active Directory discovery panel and a lab for iteratively developing and testing malware against Sophos, CrowdStrike, and Windows Defender endpoint detection and response agents.
  • The framework used multiple AI agents with distinct roles, including a Claude Opus 4.5 agent that coordinated the research and development process.
  • Sophos found no evidence that AI was embedded in deployed malware or operating independently in victim environments, and said the technology was used to accelerate payload development, testing, and refinement.

Over 116,000 Mincraft systems infected in WeedHack malware campaign — Should Read

A malware-as-a-service campaign called WeedHack has infected over 116,000 Minecraft players by spreading malicious mods, clients, cheats, and utilities through YouTube links and SEO-poisoned sites. The infostealer steals Minecraft sessions, browser credentials, crypto wallet data, and other accounts, with paid tiers adding remote access features.

  • The WeedHack malware campaign has infected 116,464 systems since January, with most victims located in the United States, Germany, India, and the United Kingdom.
  • WeedHack spreads through malicious Minecraft mods, clients, cheats, and utilities promoted via YouTube videos and SEO poisoning.
  • McAfee reported more than 240 distribution URLs and 3,820 unique malicious JAR files associated with the campaign.
  • The free WeedHack stealer targets Minecraft session IDs, browser cookies and saved passwords, cryptocurrency add-ons and wallet apps, Discord, Steam, and Telegram credentials, and can capture screenshots.
  • WeedHack offers a premium tier that adds remote control with mouse and keyboard input, webcam access, a keylogger, remote shell, and remote file management.

Pakistan-Linked SideCopy Targets Afghanistan Finance Ministry with Xeno RAT — Should Read

Pakistan-linked SideCopy is targeting Afghanistan’s Ministry of Finance and related government staff with Pashto-language spear-phishing that deploys Xeno RAT. The campaign uses malicious LNK/HTA execution, registry persistence, and a DLL loader to steal data and maintain remote access.

  • Cybersecurity researchers disclosed a spear-phishing campaign likely conducted by the Pakistan-aligned SideCopy group against Afghanistan's Ministry of Finance using the open-source Xeno RAT.
  • The campaign, called Operation XENOFISCAL, also targets provincial revenue and finance directorates, Pashto-speaking government officials, and provincial-level government employees.
  • The attack begins with a ZIP archive containing a malicious LNK file with a Pashto-language filename, reflecting the attacker's familiarity with the target environment.
  • When executed, the LNK file uses mshta.exe to fetch a remote HTA from a compromised Afghan education domain and run obfuscated JavaScript in memory.
  • The malware establishes Registry-based persistence, drops Xeno RAT 1.8.7 and a decoy document via a DLL-based loader, and can perform activities including file operations, keylogging, screenshots, clipboard monitoring, and webcam or microphone tracking.

White House unveils pared-back AI executive order — Should Read

The White House issued a scaled-back AI executive order that creates voluntary government testing and cybersecurity collaboration for frontier AI models, with reviews within 30 days of public release instead of stricter pre-release controls. The order emphasizes industry partnership and critical infrastructure security while avoiding mandatory licensing or preclearance requirements.

  • The White House released a pared-back artificial intelligence executive order after an earlier version was scrapped amid internal dissent.
  • The new order changes the voluntary government review period for AI models to within 30 days of public release, instead of the previously mandated 90 days.
  • The order says the voluntary framework does not authorize mandatory government licensing, preclearance, or permitting requirements for developing, publishing, releasing, or distributing new AI models.
  • The executive order directs the Treasury Department to lead creation of an AI cybersecurity clearinghouse for collaboration among government, industry, and critical infrastructure operators.
  • The order calls for industry and government to select trusted partners to access designated covered frontier models for improving critical infrastructure cybersecurity and tracking cyber threats in a classified setting.

Wardriving assessment across Mexico: Preparing for the 2026 World Cup — Should Read

Kaspersky’s wardriving study of Mexico City, Guadalajara, and Monterrey ahead of the 2026 World Cup found that while most Wi-Fi networks use WPA2/WPA3, many still expose users through open networks, enabled WPS, default or revealing SSIDs, and predictable ISP/router configurations. Travelers and operators should avoid unsafe public Wi-Fi practices and reduce exposure by disabling WPS, changing default SSIDs, and using stronger modern wireless configurations.

  • Kaspersky GReAT conducted a passive wardriving assessment in Mexico City, Guadalajara, and Monterrey to analyze wireless environments around 2026 FIFA World Cup host areas, tourist zones, airports, and stadiums.
  • The study recorded 84,588 wireless signals with 69,473 unique SSIDs, with Mexico City accounting for 61.4% of signals, Guadalajara for 23.6%, and Monterrey for 14.8%.
  • Approximately 34% of detected networks retained default manufacturer or ISP SSID naming conventions, and more than 30% of networks in all three cities reused the access point MAC address as the visible SSID.
  • WPA2 was the dominant authentication mechanism, but insecure open networks still accounted for between 10% and 12% of detected wireless infrastructure across the three cities.
  • WPS remained widely exposed, with 45% of all detected access points advertising WPS capabilities and around half of WPA2/WPA3-secured deployments still having WPS enabled.

Microsoft Exchange Online outage causes email delays, failures — May Read

Microsoft is investigating a widespread Exchange Online outage causing significant email delays and delivery failures for customers in North America and Germany. The incident affects mail flow and access, with some messages remaining undelivered for over an hour.

  • Microsoft is working to address a widespread service issue affecting the Exchange Online mail flow pipeline for customers across North America and Germany.
  • Microsoft first acknowledged the incident, tracked as EX1331830, at 10:33 EDT after investigating reports from users on social media.
  • Affected users are experiencing significant delays in sending and receiving email messages, with some messages remaining undelivered for over an hour.
  • Some users are seeing temporary SMTP deferral errors, including messages about exceeded concurrent connection limits and abrupt connection closures.
  • Microsoft classified the service outage as an incident and said engineers are reviewing customer reports to determine the root cause and next troubleshooting steps.

Instagram users locked out after Meta AI abused to steal accounts — May Read

Attackers hijacked high-value Instagram accounts by tricking Meta’s AI-powered recovery system with AI-generated selfie videos, allowing them to change account emails and bypass protections like 2FA. Affected users reported being unable to recover accounts because Meta’s support process kept them stuck in automated chatbot loops without human escalation.

  • Multiple Instagram users reported that their accounts were hijacked after attackers convinced Meta’s AI-powered support tools that they were the legitimate owners.
  • The reported takeover method involved starting Instagram’s hacked-account recovery flow, using an AI-generated video based on a target’s account photo to pass selfie verification, and then changing the account’s associated email address.
  • Some affected users said the method bypassed protections such as two-factor authentication and left them unable to recover access through Meta’s automated chatbot support process.
  • Impacted accounts included rare or high-value accounts such as one previously used by the Obama White House team, Jane Manchun Wong’s account, @hey, and @korn.
  • Meta vice president of communications Andy Stone said on social media that the issue had been resolved and that Meta was securing impacted accounts.

Russia claims foreign spy agencies hacked officials' phones — May Read

Russia’s FSB claims foreign intelligence agencies hacked senior officials’ phones to steal communications, location data and audio/video, allegedly using infrastructure tied to major tech companies. The agency provided no technical evidence or named spyware, and companies such as Cloudflare and Fastly were not accused of direct involvement.

  • Russia's Federal Security Service accused foreign intelligence services of conducting a large-scale espionage operation against senior Russian officials' mobile devices.
  • The FSB alleged the malware could extract data, intercept communications, access geolocation and contact lists, and conduct covert audio and video surveillance.
  • Russian authorities opened a criminal investigation to identify the perpetrators, map the infrastructure used, and determine the scope of compromised information.
  • The FSB did not name the spyware, explain how devices were infected, or provide technical evidence supporting its claims.
  • TASS reported that FSB-distributed footage included offices linked to Cloudflare and Fastly, but neither company was directly accused of participating in the alleged operation.

Dashlane Discloses Brute-Force Attack, Encrypted Vaults of Fewer Than 20 Users Downloaded — May Read

Dashlane says an external brute-force campaign against some user accounts succeeded in fewer than 20 cases, allowing attackers to download encrypted password vaults. The company says the vaults remain protected by users’ Master Passwords and has notified affected users directly.

  • Dashlane disclosed that fewer than 20 users on its personal subscription plan had their encrypted vaults downloaded after a brute-force attack by an unknown external threat actor.
  • The May 31, 2026 attack targeted certain Dashlane user accounts with the aim of breaking two-factor authentication protections and registering new devices on existing accounts.
  • Dashlane said the high volume of attempts triggered temporary account suspensions and authentication issues due to its built-in security controls.
  • Dashlane directly notified each user whose vault was at risk and said users who did not receive a specific vault-risk message were not impacted.
  • Dashlane advised users to review registered devices, remove unrecognized devices, enable two-factor authentication, and use a strong Master Password.

Why the browser is now the front line for AI security — May Read

AI is accelerating both phishing attacks and unmanaged employee use of AI tools, and both risks increasingly happen inside the browser where traditional email, endpoint, and blocklist-based defenses have limited visibility. The article argues that browser-based security controls are becoming essential for detecting AI-enabled attacks, governing AI app usage, monitoring OAuth grants, and preventing sensitive data exposure.

  • The article argues that both AI-enabled attacks and uncontrolled employee adoption of AI tools are playing out primarily inside browser sessions.
  • Push Security says AI is helping attackers create and iterate phishing-as-a-service tools faster, including techniques such as ClickFix, InstallFix, ConsentFix, and device code phishing.
  • The article states that traditional indicator-based detections are degraded because phishing domains are often short-lived and attackers increasingly use legitimate sites and non-email delivery channels such as malvertising and search results.
  • The article says employees are using unapproved AI apps, AI browser extensions, and AI-connected OAuth integrations, creating risks from sensitive data uploads, clipboard pastes, extension data collection, and broad consent grants.
  • The article presents Push Security as a browser-based threat detection and response platform that provides browser-layer telemetry for both emerging attack detection and AI governance.

AI-Driven Exploitation is Destroying Vulnerability Management. Here’s How to Handle It. — Optional

AI is shrinking the time from vulnerability disclosure to real-world exploitation to hours, while enterprise patching still takes weeks. Organizations need to prioritize likely exploited flaws, rapidly validate exposure, and use temporary mitigations to buy time for safe remediation.

  • AI-driven exploitation is compressing the window between vulnerability disclosure and indiscriminate internet-wide exploitation to hours rather than days.
  • The article argues that patching alone is no longer sufficient because enterprise remediation is constrained by uptime requirements, testing, change windows, approvals, and compliance obligations.
  • Anthropic’s Project Glasswing update said Anthropic and about 50 partners used Claude Mythos Preview to identify more than 10,000 high- or critical-severity vulnerabilities in systemically important software in a single month.
  • The article recommends that defenders preemptively prioritize vulnerabilities most likely to be exploited, rapidly validate whether their own environments are exposed, and apply temporary mitigations while normal patching proceeds.
  • The article presents the watchTowr Platform as an AI-powered preemptive exposure management solution designed to identify exploitable weaknesses, validate emerging threat exposure, and autonomously mitigate risk before compromise.

How Leading Organizations Are Turning EDR Into Operational Resilience — Optional

EDR alone is not enough for operational resilience; organizations need to pair endpoint visibility with proactive hardening and 24/7 managed detection and response. The article argues that combining Bitdefender GravityZone PHASR, GravityZone EDR, and MDR can reduce attacker opportunity, alert fatigue, and response delays for lean security teams.

  • Many mid-sized organizations have adopted EDR but still struggle to operationalize it because lean security teams face high alert volumes, limited monitoring capacity, skills shortages, and reactive workflows.
  • The article states that AI-enabled attacks and living-off-the-land techniques are increasing pressure on security teams, with 67% of organizations reporting more AI-powered attacks and 84% of major attacks leveraging legitimate tools and processes.
  • Bitdefender GravityZone PHASR is described as using AI to dynamically reduce risky actions, unnecessary privileges, and abuse of legitimate tools without disrupting productivity.
  • Bitdefender MDR is described as providing 24x7 monitoring, threat hunting, investigation, and rapid response to extend the capacity of internal security teams.
  • The article argues that combining GravityZone PHASR, GravityZone EDR, and Bitdefender MDR can reduce attacker opportunity, improve detection and containment, reduce alert fatigue, and strengthen cyber resilience.

item.title: Miasma npm compromise, PAN-OS exploitation, and Netlogon RCE activity
item.description: The reporting in this window highlights a threat landscape dominated by software supply-chain compromise, rapidly operationalized vulnerability exploitation, and continued abuse of trusted internet platforms for malware delivery and persistence. A second cross-cutting theme is operational strain:...
item.content:encoded:
The reporting in this window highlights a threat landscape dominated by software supply-chain compromise, rapidly operationalized vulnerability exploitation, and continued abuse of trusted internet platforms for malware delivery and persistence. A second cross-cutting theme is operational strain: defenders are contending not only with active threats, but also with visibility gaps caused by vulnerability database backlogs and authentication or collaboration platform outages. The most consequential supply-chain story was the compromise of more than 30 npm packages in Red Hat's @redhat-cloud-services namespace. Researchers said attackers likely used a compromised Red Hat employee GitHub account to inject malicious code, add a GitHub Actions workflow, and publish backdoored packages through trusted publishing. The resulting malware, dubbed Miasma, was described as a credential and secret stealer targeting developer workstations and CI/CD environments, with overlap to Mini Shai-Hulud and reported downstream impact to hundreds of GitHub repositories. Additional analysis showed the packages used a heavily obfuscated preinstall payload that decrypted implants at runtime, downloaded Bun, harvested secrets from GitHub Actions, cloud platforms, Kubernetes, Vault, SSH, Docker, GPG, and .env files, and in some cases read runner memory to recover masked CI secrets. Researchers also said the malware could enumerate writable GitHub repositories, commit malicious workflows, and establish persistence via developer tooling configuration changes. A separate npm case involved codexui-android, which Aikido said exfiltrated OpenAI Codex authentication tokens from ~/.codex/auth.json to an attacker-controlled domain. The same behavior was reportedly observed in Android apps packaging the npm component, reinforcing the broader trend of package-level compromise targeting developer credentials and machine-to-cloud trust chains. Multiple reports underscored the continued use of compromised websites as malware distribution infrastructure. Silent Push said the DriveSurge actor hijacked thousands of legitimate sites and used the zTDS traffic distribution system to profile visitors before serving either ClickFix lures or FakeUpdate prompts. The campaign impersonated updates for a wide range of browsers and also included a macOS-focused clipboard-hijacking ClickFix variant. WordPress remained a major abuse surface. GoDaddy identified malware on roughly 1,980 WordPress sites that hid command-and-control data inside Steam Community profile comments using invisible Unicode characters, then pulled JavaScript from hello-mywordl.info and deployed a PHP backdoor. In a separate WordPress threat, attackers were actively exploiting CVE-2026-8732 in WP Maps Pro to create rogue administrator accounts; Wordfence said it blocked thousands of exploitation attempts in a 24-hour period. Several reports centered on vulnerabilities moving quickly from disclosure to exploitation. Palo Alto Networks' CVE-2026-0257, an authentication bypass affecting PAN-OS and Prisma Access under specific GlobalProtect configurations, is now being exploited in the wild and was added to CISA's KEV catalog. Another high-priority development was active exploitation of CVE-2026-41089, a critical unauthenticated Netlogon remote code execution flaw affecting supported Windows Server versions. At the same time, reporting emphasized structural challenges in vulnerability visibility and prioritization. A Department of Commerce inspector general report said NIST's NVD backlog exceeded 27,000 unprocessed vulnerabilities by the end of 2025, citing poor planning and duplicated work with CISA's Vulnrichment program. Separate coverage noted that median time from CVE disclosure to exploitation has reportedly dropped to 1.6 days, while a critical Gogs RCE remained unpatched at publication. Espionage and influence operations also featured prominently. Seqrite detailed Operation Dragon Weave, a China-aligned campaign targeting government, research, academic, technology, and financial organizations in the Czech Republic and Taiwan using Rust-based tooling and Azure Blob Storage dead-drop C2. Other reporting pointed to a separate suspected China-linked intrusion using the Go-based TencShell implant, while ESET assessed China-aligned groups remained highly active globally into 2026. Check Point's roundup added that the Russia-aligned GREYVIBE group used ChatGPT and Google Gemini to support phishing, malware development, and post-compromise actions against Ukrainian targets. The same reporting referenced a destructive Iran-linked operation against LA Metro and a social-engineering-driven Carnival data breach affecting nearly 6 million people. On the disruption and governance side, Microsoft investigated separate outages affecting Office for the web and Teams file access, as well as MFA setup and the My Sign-Ins platform, with the latter tied to a cache configuration change and failover-related resource pressure. Elsewhere, Spanish police arrested a suspect accused of leaking sensitive personal data tied to multiple state institutions, and the NSA announced new leadership for its Cybersecurity Directorate and Collaboration Center amid broader organizational turbulence.

Critical Windows Netlogon RCE flaw now exploited in attacks — Must Read

Belgium’s national cybersecurity authority warns that attackers are now actively exploiting CVE-2026-41089, a critical Windows Netlogon remote code execution flaw affecting domain controllers. Organizations should urgently patch supported Windows Server systems, including Windows Server 2025, to reduce the risk of compromise.

  • The Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium warned that CVE-2026-41089, a critical Windows Netlogon flaw, is now being actively exploited in the wild.
  • Microsoft patched CVE-2026-41089 during the May 2026 Patch Tuesday and described it as a stack-based buffer overflow that can allow remote code execution on domain controllers without authentication.
  • The vulnerability affects all currently supported Windows Server versions, including Windows Server 2025.
  • The Belgian cybersecurity authority urged administrators to patch vulnerable servers immediately, but it did not provide additional details about the observed attacks.
  • Microsoft had not updated its advisory to confirm active exploitation at the time of the report, and the company did not reply to BleepingComputer’s request for confirmation.

Critical WP Maps Pro Flaw Actively Exploited to Create Admin Accounts — Must Read

Attackers are actively exploiting a critical WP Maps Pro flaw, CVE-2026-8732, to create administrator accounts and fully take over vulnerable WordPress sites. Any site running version 6.1.0 or earlier should update immediately to 6.1.1.

  • Threat actors are actively attempting to exploit a critical WP Maps Pro vulnerability to create malicious administrator accounts on vulnerable WordPress sites.
  • The flaw, tracked as CVE-2026-8732 with a CVSS score of 9.8, affects all WP Maps Pro versions up to and including 6.1.0 and was fixed in version 6.1.1.
  • According to Wordfence, the vulnerability stems from a temporary access feature that lets unauthenticated users invoke the wpgmp_temp_access_support() function and create a new WordPress administrator account.
  • The plugin maintainers released a patch on May 20, 2026, that restricts access to the vulnerable endpoint to authenticated administrators only.
  • Wordfence said it blocked 2,858 attacks targeting the issue over the past 24 hours, and urged site owners to update to the latest version.

Miasma Supply Chain Attack Compromises Red Hat npm Packages with Credential-Stealing Worm — Should Read

Several @redhat-cloud-services npm packages were compromised in a supply chain campaign called Miasma that steals developer and CI/CD secrets, exfiltrates them in encrypted form, and can spread by abusing stolen GitHub and npm credentials. Organizations that installed the affected versions should treat hosts and build artifacts as potentially compromised, rotate credentials, and check for persistence beyond simply removing the packages.

  • A supply chain campaign dubbed Miasma compromised multiple @redhat-cloud-services npm packages with a malicious preinstall hook that steals credentials and secrets from developer machines and CI/CD environments.
  • Researchers said the malware collects GitHub Actions secrets, npm tokens, cloud credentials, Kubernetes and Vault material, SSH keys, Git credentials, and other sensitive files, then exfiltrates them using encrypted logic to api.anthropic.com with GitHub as a fallback.
  • The malware can enumerate GitHub repositories writable by a stolen token, read workflow files, and commit malicious workflows through the GitHub API to support downstream propagation.
  • The article states the malware also attempts privilege escalation in CI, checks for protections such as CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Carbon Black, and StepSecurity Harden-Runner, and establishes persistence through Anthropic Claude Code and Visual Studio Code configuration changes.
  • Evidence cited in the article suggests a compromised Red Hat employee GitHub account was the initial access point used to inject malicious orphan commits into two RedHatInsights repositories, bypassing code review.

OpenAI Codex Authentication Tokens Stolen in codexui-android npm Supply Chain Attack — Should Read

A popular npm package, codexui-android, and related Android apps were found stealing OpenAI Codex authentication tokens by secretly exfiltrating the local auth.json file to an attacker-controlled server. The key takeaway is that developers using this tool may have exposed long-lived refresh tokens that can enable persistent account access.

  • Aikido Security reported that the npm package codexui-android, promoted as a remote web UI for OpenAI Codex, has been exfiltrating Codex authentication tokens to the attacker-controlled domain sentry.anyclaw.store.
  • The malicious code reads the plaintext file ~/.codex/auth.json and sends access_token, refresh_token, id_token, and account ID data to the remote server.
  • Aikido said the exfiltration was added about a month after the package was first published, and the package's GitHub repository remained clean while the npm build contained the malicious functionality.
  • The same exfiltration chain was also found in Android apps including OpenClaw Codex Claude AI Agent and Codex, which run the npm package inside a PRoot sandbox and send credentials after users sign in.
  • The article also notes separate Aikido findings that deleted Google API keys can remain usable for up to 23 minutes, and that Google has since decided to treat the issue as a P0 bug.

WordPress malware campaign hides payloads in Steam profiles — Should Read

Hackers infected nearly 2,000 WordPress sites with malware that hid its command-and-control payload inside Steam Community profile comments using invisible Unicode characters. The campaign let attackers quietly load malicious JavaScript and maintain a PHP backdoor while blending in with normal WordPress and Steam traffic.

  • GoDaddy researchers found malware on about 1,980 WordPress websites in a campaign that used Steam Community profile comments to hide command-and-control data.
  • The malware encoded payloads with six invisible Unicode characters embedded in benign-looking Steam comments, allowing visible text to act as camouflage.
  • The decoded payload built a URL to hello-mywordl.info, which served JavaScript disguised as legitimate library files and injected it into every frontend WordPress page.
  • The final stage installed a PHP backdoor that accepted base64-encoded code through POST requests when a specific authentication cookie was present.
  • GoDaddy said defenders should look for references to Steam Community URLs, suspicious external JavaScript injections, invisible Unicode characters, and requests involving hello-mywordl.info or the malware’s authentication cookies.

Microsoft fixes KB5089549 Windows security update install issues — Should Read

Microsoft fixed the Windows 11 KB5089549 install failures caused by low EFI System Partition space in the KB5089573 preview update, with the broader fix rolling out in June Patch Tuesday updates. Affected users can also use Microsoft's workaround or Known Issue Rollback if they haven’t installed the newer update yet.

  • Microsoft resolved a known issue that caused installation failures and 0x800f0922 errors when deploying the May 2026 Windows 11 security update KB5089549.
  • Microsoft said the failures were caused by insufficient free space on the EFI System Partition, especially on devices with 10 MB or less available, and the update could fail during reboot at around 35 to 36 percent completion.
  • The issue was fixed in the Windows 11 KB5089573 preview cumulative update released on May 26, 2026, and the fix will also be included in the June Patch Tuesday updates.
  • Users who do not install the optional May 2026 update can mitigate the problem with Known Issue Rollback, and enterprise admins can deploy a Group Policy workaround provided by Microsoft.
  • Microsoft noted that affected users may see a "Something didn't go as planned. Undoing changes." message and log entries such as "SpaceCheck" and "ServicingBootFiles failed" when the update rolls back.

Hackers hijack thousands of sites for ClickFix and FakeUpdate attacks — Should Read
